Leviticus 27 concludes the book with something that might seem mundane but strikes at the deepest question of the human heart: What are we willing to give to God? The chapter addresses vows—the voluntary consecration of people, animals, land, and possessions to the Lord—and establishes a careful system for valuing what people pledge. Notice what God does here: He doesn't shame those who make vows they cannot fully keep. When someone is too poor to pay what they've vowed, the priest adjusts the value downward, allowing even the struggling to honor their commitment. This reveals something profound about God's character. He receives vows seriously, but He also makes room for human limitation. He doesn't demand the impossible; He demands faithfulness within the bounds of what we actually are.

Yet the chapter also contains a sobering line that cannot be softened: what is "devoted" to the Lord—completely set apart rather than merely vowed—cannot be redeemed (verse 28). This especially includes people set apart for destruction (verse 29), a command that haunts us because it points to the gravity of God's holiness and the reality of judgment. Not everything we give to God is negotiable. Some things, once fully consecrated, belong to Him alone. The firstborn animals belong to Him by His sovereign claim, not by our choice. The tithe is His by divine right. This is the counterweight to the grace of adjustable vows—God will not be bargained with about what He has claimed as His own.

The repeated phrase "according to the sanctuary shekel" anchors every valuation in God's standard, not ours. We cannot reimagine what our vows are worth or what we owe. Yet the permission to redeem—to buy back what we've vowed by adding a fifth to its value—shows that God leaves room for those who change their minds or face hardship. He is not a tyrant demanding the sacrifice of all we have. But He is also not a merchant waiting to haggle. He sets the terms. The question these regulations pose to every reader remains: What am I actually willing to give, not just in moments of emotional commitment, but in the sustained reality of following God? Do I view my possessions, my time, my future as truly belonging to Him, or do I merely offer Him what I can spare?
